text:
Admission Date:  1992-03-17              Discharge Date:   1991-03-01

Date of Birth:  2004-02-27             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Penicillins / Lidocaine / Latex / ceftriaxone

Attending:Adam
Chief Complaint:
Abdominal pain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Central line
Arterial line


History of Present Illness:
60yo female with history of MS Craig Fletcher d/o who presents with
chief complaint of restlessness. Per triage note, patient was
brought in from rehabilitation center for abdominal pain. Pt
reportedly had a sexual encounter with her Diaz, who is
also a resident at rehab, yesterday that involved "rough sex."
Patient is unable to give details of this event, but states it
was consensual. She denies any vaginal pain, vaginal bleeding,
rectal bleeding. She denies any other symptoms except for mild
headache. She states she is here because she does not want to
feel restless anymore.

On arrival to the ED overnight, initial VS: T- 99.5, HR- 100,
BP- 112/72, RR- 16, SaO2- 97% on room air. Labs were notable for
WBC 19.2 with left shift, lactate 4.5. U/A showing moderate
bacteria, > 182 wbc, large leukocyte esterase, positive nitrite.
Stox negative. Serum tylenol 6. Depakote level 69. CXR showed
LLL opacity, likely atelectasis and CT head demonstrated diffuse
global atrophy with no acute intracranial process. CT abdomen
showed "subtle loss of the corticomedullary differentiation of
the left kidney with delayed excretion raises concern for
pyelonephritis. Mild left hydroureteronephrosis with an apparent
intraluminal filling defect in the distal ureter which is
non-specific and may represent debris, a non-calcified, stone,
and soft tissue also remains a possibility. Thick-walled bladder
compatible with clinically known cystitis."

After CT with contrast, pt became hypotensive, developed pain,
and mottled skin. Per ED, "presumably anaphylaxis." Given epi,
benedryl, steroids, pepcid and symptoms resolved. Overall, in
ED, patient received ativan, morphine, zofran, ceftriaxone,
epinephrine, solumedrol, famotidine, benadryl. She received 3L
NS, and repeat lactate was 3.6.

Vitals on transfer to the floor were T- 98.0, HR- 114, BP-
126/67, RR- 26, SaO2- 100% on 3L NC. This morning she was found
to have SBP in the low 90s with some somnolence as the patient
was only responsive to sternal rub. She received 3L NS with
improvement in mental status and SBP to 100s. HR remained in
high 90s/low 100s. She was afebrile with no acute complaints.
She is being transferred to the ICU for concern of urosepsis.

Past Medical History:
-multiple sclerosis
-secondary depression and psychosis
-neurogenic bladder
-sleep apnea
-Fletcher disorder

Social History:
Lives at Sun Behavioral Columbus Rehab 12-26 being wheelchair bound from
progressive MS
Craig Diaz at rehab, consensual sexual intercourse, no
concerns for abuse
Used to work at Uva Culpeper Hospital with Pediatric early intervention program
Denies EtOH, tobacco or illicit drug use

Family History:
No family history of MS

Physical Exam:
ADMISSION EXAM:
General: Somnolent but responsive.
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear, EOMI
Neck: supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD
CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs,
gallops
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales,
ronchi
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present,
no organomegaly
GU: no foley
Ext: warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or
edema
Neuro: Oriented to person and place. CNII-XII intact, 09-25
strength upper/lower extremities, grossly normal sensation, 2+
reflexes bilaterally, gait deferred, finger-to-nose intact

DISCHARGE EXAM:
Vitals:  Tmax 99.3, 98-112/64-72; 90-97; 18; 95%RA
General: Alert, awake, responsive and interactive, oriented to
person, time, and place, occasionally thinks she is at rehab.
able to count days of the week backwards from Sunday to Tuesday
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear, has
intraophthalmoplegia
Neck: supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD
CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs,
gallops
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales,
ronchi
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present,
no organomegaly
GU: foley draining clear urine
Ext: warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or
edema
Neuro: Oriented to person, but not to place or time. CNII-XII
intact, 2/5 strength in bilateral lower extremities, 09-25
strength in upper extremities except left elbow flexor. grossly
reduced sensation over lower extremities, gait deferred

CT Abd/Pelvis 10-17
IMPRESSION:
1. Loss of the normal corticomedullary differentiation of the
left kidney
which raises concern for possible pyelonephritis.
2. Delayed excretion of contrast involving the left kidney. Mild
left
hydroureteronephrosis demonstrated to the level of distal ureter
where there is an apparent filling defect which non-specific and
may represent a stone, debris, or even possibly soft tissue.
3. Thick-walled enhancing bladder compatible with clinically
known cystitis.

CT head 10-17:
IMPRESSION:
1. Motion-limited examination demonstrating no acute
intracranial hemorrhage or mass effect.
2. Whit ematter hypodense foci- likely non-specific. However,
correlate
clinically to decide on the need for further workup with MRI if
not
contra-indicated.

1991-03-01 Renal US:
The right kidney measures 12 cm in size and the left kidney
measures 12 cm in size. There is normal corticomedullary
echotexture.  Ectasia of the left renal pelvis is seen. There is
no evidence of hydronephrosis or nephrolithiasis.
The urinary bladder appears within normal limits. The ureteral
jets are seen bilaterally.

Brief Hospital Course:
60yo female with history of MS, neurogenic bladder and [**Month/Day/Year **]
disorder who presents from rehab with altered mental status,
found to have E. coli UTI, bacteremia and sepsis, resolved with
antibiotics.

1. E. coli UTI, bacteremia, and sepsis- Patient presented with
altered mental status, elevated WBC, elevated lactate and
positive UA.   Imaging concerning for pyelonephritis.  She
received ceftriaxone in the ED but developed possible
anaphylaxis.   Initially treated in the MICU given sepsis with
temperature of 101 and tachycardic to the 130s.  Meropenem and
Vancomycin started in the MICU. The urology service was
consulted and they determined that the only indication for
drainage would be if there is an obstruction; she has chronic
hydronephrosis due to neurogenic bladder and reflux. An arterial
line and a central venous access line were placed and patient
given fluids. Her urine and blood cultures both resulted E.coli
sensitive to fluoroquinolones. Her vancomycin and meropenem were
discontinued and switched to cipro to complete a 14-day
treatment course (last day 1987-09-14).  UTI appears related to
sexual intercourse and neurogenic bladder.  Consider post-coital
prophylaxis.

2. Altered mental status- Pt was initially lethargic upon her
admission to the MICU. She was extremely agitated on the night
of 10-17 and she was given seroquel and haldol which eventually
calmed her down. Her mental status waxed and waned during her
stay in the MICU and she was re-started on her home wellbutrin
and divalproex. Pt had a negative head CT. After transfer to the
floor, patient continues to have waxing and Wheeler mental
status, but slowly improved over time.  AAOx3 on discharge.
Trazodone held in the setting of altered mental status, consider
restarting as outpatient when stable.

3. Possible Anaphylaxis- Patient became hypotensive to the high
70s while undergoing CT scan in the ED after receiving
ceftriaxone. She received benadryl, steroid, famotidine and
epinephrine with reponse in BP. Rash resolved by the time of her
admission to the MICU. Unclear if this is secondary to contrast
vs ceftriaxone the patient received in the ED as she has a known
penicillin allergy.

4. Multiple sclerosis- patient takes Glatiramer for relapsing
MS. She is not on any other chronic medications (interferon beta
1a, natalizumab). She is taking baclofen and detrol for history
of neurogenic bladder. Copoxone was initially held in the
setting of sepsis. Restarted on discharge.  Her baclofen and
detrol were continued at her home dose.

5. Neurogenic bladder- Patient has incontinence at baseline
without urinary retention.  Detrol held in the setting of UTI
and sepsis.  Consider restarting as outpatient.

6. Depression, Stuart disorder - Pt was periodically with
altered mental status and agitation during her stay in the MICU.
Her home buproprion and divalproex were continued.

7. Hyperlipidemia- continued on her home statin

# Transitional issues-
Pending HIV screening results, blood culture, urine culture
(will communicate to Dr. Gentry
Restart Detrol after UTI treatment
Restart Trazodone once mental status completely back to baseline
Consider starting Bactrim DS for postcoital prophylaxis

Medications on Admission:
bupropion 100mg Frye Regional Medical Center
divalproex 500mg qhs, 500mg qam, 250mg @1600
simethicone 40mg TID PRN
artificial tears
detrol LA 2mg qam, 2mg qhs
copaxone 20mg qam injection
trazadone 25mg qhs PRN
pravastatin 40mg qhs
baclofen 20mg Frye Regional Medical Center
tylenol 650mg Frye Regional Medical Center

times a day).
2. divalproex 250 mg Tablet Extended Release 24 hr Sig: as
directed Tablet Extended Release 24 hr PO three times a day:
500mg qHS, 500mg qAM, 250mg at 1600.
3. simethicone 40 mg Strip Sig: One (1)  PO three times a day as
needed for indigestion.
4. Artificial Tears  Ophthalmic

needed for insomnia.
7. pravastatin 40 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO at bedtime.
8. baclofen 10 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO BID (2 times a
day).
9. Tylenol 8 Hour 650 mg Tablet Extended Release Sig: One (1)
Tablet Extended Release PO twice a day.
10. Ciprofloxacin 500mg Frye Regional Medical Center (last day 1987-09-14)

Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
White Memorial Medical Center for the Aged - LTC

Discharge Diagnosis:
PRIMARY:
UTI
Pyelonephritis
Sepsis
E. coli bacteremia


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Out of Bed with assistance to chair or
wheelchair.


Discharge Instructions:
Ms. Malone,

It was a pleasure taking care of you during your Baylor Scott & White Medical Center - College Station
hospitalization.  You were admitted because you had an infection
in your urinary tract and blood stream.  We treated this with
antibiotics (Bactrim), which you will continue till 1987-09-14.

We made the following changes to your medications:
STARTED Ciprofloxacin twice daily (last day 1987-09-14)
STOPPED Detrol (please discuss restarting this with Dr. Gentry
STOPPED Trazodone (please discuss restarting this with Dr. Gentry

Followup Instructions:
Follow up at Sun Behavioral Columbus Rehab with Dr. Gentry
